name: Out-of-SSA Translation
summary: Removing SSA before register allocation: parallel copies on incoming edges, sequentialised by Boissinot's algorithm to handle the swap problem. Boissinot 2009 showed SSA-based coalescing before destruction beats coalescing after.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
SSA must be removed before register allocation in most backends because physical registers cannot be assigned in single-assignment form. The naïve "replace each `φ(x₁, x₂)` with copies on the incoming edges" approach is subtly wrong — parallel copies at block boundaries can conflict (the swap problem: `x = y; y = x` requires a temporary if both copies must happen logically simultaneously).

Sreedhar et al. (POPL 1999) formalised out-of-SSA as a parallel-copy insertion problem on CFG edges, followed by parallel-copy sequentialisation. The sequentialisation handles cycles via a temporary register when no free register is available.

sources.label: Translating Out of SSA Form (Sreedhar et al., POPL 1999)

sections.title: The parallel-copy problem
sections.content:
On each predecessor edge of a φ-function, the φ's incoming value for that predecessor must be copied into the φ's destination. When multiple φ-functions share a predecessor, all those copies execute *in parallel* logically — but a sequential code generator must order them.

The pathological case is a swap: φ₁ takes x in this predecessor, φ₂ takes y, and the destinations are y and x respectively. Naïve sequential copying (`y = x; x = y`) loses x. Boissinot et al. (2009) and earlier work show how to topologically sort the copy graph: copies form chains and cycles, chains sequence directly, cycles need one temporary register to break the cycle.

A related issue: when a copy's source register is later in the sequence overwritten by another copy whose source is the same register, the order matters. The resolution algorithm reads from a 'before' map and writes to an 'after' map, producing a dependency DAG plus cycles, then linearises the DAG and breaks cycles with a temporary.

sections.title: SSA-based coalescing before destruction
sections.content:
Boissinot, Darte, Rastello, Guillon (2009) showed that doing **SSA-based coalescing before destruction** produces better code than coalescing after. While SSA structure is still explicit, live ranges can be merged across φ-functions when the source and destination do not interfere. This eliminates copies that a post-SSA coalescer would have to reconstruct from less information.

Cranelift's regalloc2 integrates this insight by operating directly on SSA, doing coalescing-aware live-range splitting and parallel-move resolution at block boundaries in one pass. The combined design avoids both the worst Chaitin spill cases and the worst classical out-of-SSA copies.

sources.label: Sreedhar et al. — Translating Out of SSA Form
Boissinot et al. — Revisiting Out-of-SSA Translation
